text: The laparoscopic camera enters through the umbilical port, revealing the gallbladder and a smooth liver surface, with the left lobe appearing normal.

The surgeon observes a slightly distended stomach due to insufflation. The colon is visible, with the tinea coli identifiable. There are some adhesions present, and the pelvis appears empty, lacking internal genital organs.

The surgeon establishes additional ports along the border of the liver, moving up to the falciform ligament. The reflection of light is visible on the liver surface as the surgeon presses to identify the proper location for port placement before making an incision.

The surgeon inserts a cannula using a screwing motion through the skin, entering the peritoneal cavity under direct vision. The trocar is removed, and the next step involves positioning on the right side along the anterior axillary line.
A 5mm port is established for the fundal grasper, followed by the placement of a port for the infinitum holder.
The surgeon identifies omental attachments and begins to shear them down, pulling the tissue away from surrounding structures.

The surgeon identifies the lymph node near the lung and proceeds to the infundibulum and neck of the gallbladder. Gentle removal of inflammatory attachments is performed, revealing the common bile duct (CBD).
The surgeon identifies the duodenum forming a C-shape and the common bile duct (CBD) in the surgical field. The focus is on the Calot's triangle area for dissection, avoiding the CBD.

The surgeon carefully removes the thickened omental attachments from the gallbladder, which appears slippery. As the dissection progresses, the tissue planes begin to open up, revealing the underlying structures.

The surgeon identifies the cystic artery and its posterior branch, noting the presence of a lymph node near the lung. The tissue appears thicker than expected, requiring careful examination to differentiate the cystic artery from surrounding structures.
The surgeon performs dissection from behind the peritoneum, pulling off fibrinous threads that resemble fabric fibers, gradually clarifying the anatomy.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and the common bile duct (CBD), preparing to achieve the critical view of safety by dissecting up to the lower part of the gallbladder fossa.

The surgeon has dissected the force and plate, revealing small attachments and vessels. These structures are visible within the surgical field, and the decision is made whether to leave them intact or proceed with further dissection.
The surgeon clears fascia and fuzzy tissue from both sides of the surgical field, creating two distinct windows for better visualization and access.
The surgeon has reached the gallbladder fossa, revealing two distinct views or windows of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the cystic artery and cystic duct, along with a lymph node, in the surgical field. The structures are clearly visible, allowing for safe clamping.
The surgeon prepares to cut the cystic duct and artery after securing them with clips, ensuring a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct and begins mobilizing the gallbladder, preparing to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon approaches the summit of the gallbladder fundus, preparing for further dissection or mobilization.
The surgeon removes the gallbladder after securing clips in place, with the fossa clearly visible on the right side of the surgical field.
